raw_consumer_statement:
I was a loyal XXXX customer since XXXX with a long, positive history on my XXXX store card. That account was never affected by fraud. Because of my good standing, XXXX later upgraded me to a XXXX XXXX XXXX card. 

XXXX on my accounts began in XX/XX/XXXX, before I ever received the XXXX card. A few months later, around XX/XX/XXXX to XX/XX/XXXX, I accepted the XXXX upgrade in good faith, believing it would give me stronger security. Instead, the fraud carried over and escalated, including XXXX XXXX and food orders. 

XXXX responded by issuing replacement cards over and over, but the fraud never stopped. At first, they told me the problem should not occur again, but they did not freeze my account. Later, they began freezing it and required me to wait 714 days for new PINs. This disrupted me but did not stop the fraudster. 

I specifically asked XXXX to cancel my online account access so the fraudster could not shop online. After that, I had no online access and had to rely on mailed statements and in-store payments. Each time I paid in the store, staff scanned my ID and printed only a small receipt showing my balance. XXXX sales rep even asked me why I was paying if the charges werent mine and told me I should get an attorney. 

By XXXX, my minimum payment had been raised as high as {$490.00}, even though much of the balance was fraudulent. XXXX combined fraudulent and legitimate charges, added high fees and interest, and still demanded payment. On XX/XX/XXXX, their XXXX XXXX Department acknowledged my claims but insisted I continue paying the portion not in dispute. 

I was billed for an inflated balance of about {$8700.00} that does not belong to me. That balance reflects fraud, not my spending. I continued paying high fees, high rates, and high minimum payments in good faith until I could no longer keep up. My loyalty and patience were not respected, my credit was damaged, and I was left carrying debt that was not mine.
